> **Interpretation.** A positive extrinsic $L_e(u_k) = 0.6$ strengthens the belief that $u_k = +1$. After this extrinsic is interleaved and fed to SISO-2, the partner decoder's prior becomes more confident, and its own extrinsic will likewise carry refined information back, boosting the overall APP on subsequent iterations.

---

### 5.3 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in turbo-decoding questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract** $L_c y_s$ and $L(u_k)$ from the APP when computing $L_e$ — yielding a value that is ~2× too large. *(−2 marks)*
> 2. **Confusing MAP and ML decoding** — MAP maximizes $P(u \mid y)$, not $P(y \mid u)$. Writing the wrong Bayes target loses the conceptual link. *(−1 mark)*
> 3. **Wrong initial conditions** — Setting $\alpha_0(s) = 1/N$ instead of $\alpha_0(s_0) = 1$ at the *known* start state breaks the forward recursion. *(−1 mark)*
> 4. **Omitting the log-domain conversion** — Writing $\prod \gamma$ in the natural domain instead of $\sum \log \gamma$ loses the "why log-MAP exists" point. *(−1 mark)*
> 5. **Treating the interleaver as a deterministic block** without explaining its role in *decorrelating the two encoders' evidence*. *(−1 mark)*

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Turbo code origin:** Berrou, Glavieux, Thitimajshima (1993), ICC paper — near-Shannon-limit performance at $10^{-5}$ BER.
- **Architecture:** PCCC = 2 × RSC + interleaver + puncturing/mux.
- **Decoder structure:** Two SISO modules linked by interleaver/deinterleaver, exchanging extrinsic LLRs.
- **Turbo principle identity:** $L(\hat{u}_k) = L_c\, y_{k,s} + L(u_k) + L_e(u_k)$.
- **Channel reliability:** $L_c = 4 a E_s/N_0 = 4 a R E_b/N_0$.
- **BCJR three pillars:** Branch metric $\gamma$, forward $\alpha$, backward $\beta$.
- **Extrinsic formula:** $L_e(u_k) = \log \dfrac{\sum_{u_k=+1}\alpha\gamma\beta}{\sum_{u_k=-1}\alpha\gamma\beta} - L(u_k) - L_c y_{k,s}$.
- **Log-domain implementation:** Uses $\max^*(x,y) = \max(x,y) + \ln(1+e^{-\vert x-y \vert})$ for numerical stability.
- **Log-MAP vs Max-Log-MAP:** Log-MAP is optimal; Max-Log-MAP is simpler but loses ~0.3–0.5 dB.
- **Typical iterations:** 6–18 — diminishing returns beyond this.
- **Stopping criteria:** Fixed iteration count, LLR sign convergence, or hard-decision stability.
- **Applications:** 3G/UMTS, LTE (initial releases), CCSDS deep-space, DVB-RCS.
- **Successor:** LDPC codes (decoded via belief propagation) — same extrinsic-information philosophy, different graphical structure.
- **Key insight:** Turbo decoding is *not* a single algorithm but an *architecture* — any two SISO modules that satisfy the additive decomposition can be combined iteratively.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Distance Properties of Turbo Codes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST414 - CODING THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Distance Properties of Turbo Codes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Turbo Codes** are a class of high-performance forward error correction (FEC) codes introduced by Berrou, Glavieux, and Thitimajshima in **1993**, formed by the **parallel concatenation** of two or more **Recursive Systematic Convolutional (RSC)** encoders separated by a **pseudorandom interleaver**. The *distance properties* of turbo codes describe the **weight distribution** (or **distance spectrum**) of all valid codewords emanating from the encoder, characterized mathematically by the **Input-Output Weight Enumerating Function (IOWEF)** and the **free Hamming distance** $d_{\text{free}}$.

The distance spectrum is given by the enumeration:

$$\{A_{w,d}\}_{w=1, d=d_{\text{free}}}^{w=N, \, d=d_{\text{max}}}$$

where $A_{w,d}$ is the **multiplicity** (number of codewords) generated by an input information word of Hamming weight $w$ producing a codeword of Hamming weight $d$, and $N$ is the information block length.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> The distance spectrum is the single most important performance-defining property of a turbo code. It governs both the **error floor** (at high SNR) and the **asymptotic bit-error-rate slope** (at low-to-medium SNR) under maximum-likelihood (ML) decoding.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine you are a **postmaster** managing two parallel postal sorting systems. A bag of letters arrives (the **information sequence**). One sorter reads the letters in the *original* order and produces a summary checklist (the **first parity stream**). The same bag is then given a *deliberate random shuffle* (the **interleaver**) and sent to a *second* sorter, producing another checklist (the **second parity stream**). The original letters themselves are also forwarded (the **systematic output**).

Now, suppose an adversary ("noise") tampers with a few letters. To detect tampering, you need to know **how many different "shapes" of damaged output the system can produce, and how often each shape occurs**. This catalog of shapes and their frequencies is the **distance spectrum**.

Key insight: The interleaver's randomness ensures that *low-weight input sequences* cannot simultaneously produce *low-weight parity outputs* from both RSC encoders. This is the secret of turbo codes' excellent distance.

## 1.3 Key Metrics Summary

| Symbol | Definition | Typical Range |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $N$ | Information block length (interleaver size) | $40$ to $65536$ |
| $K$ | Constraint length of RSC encoder | $3$ to $5$ |
| $R_c$ | Overall code rate | $1/2$ or $1/3$ |
| $d_{\text{free}}$ | Free Hamming distance | $6$ to $20+$ |
| $A_{w,d}$ | Multiplicity of codewords of weight $d$ from inputs of weight $w$ | Depends on $N$ |
| $w_{\min}$ | Minimum input weight that produces low-weight codewords | $2$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the free distance is bounded:** A weight-1 input sequence in an RSC encoder produces an *infinite* weight parity stream (due to the recursive feedback), so $w=1$ input patterns never produce low-weight codewords. The *lowest-weight* codewords originate from $w=2$ inputs that hit a *low-weight* parity from one RSC and a *finite-weight* parity from the other.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constituent Encoder Transfer Function

The **Augmented State Diagram** of an RSC encoder is used to derive its **conditional Input-Output Weight Enumerating Function (CWEF)** $A^{C_i}_c(w, D)$, which enumerates the parity output weight $d$ for each input weight $w$:

$$A^{C}_c(w, D) \;\triangleq\; \sum_{d} A^{C}_{w,d}\, D^{d}$$

The full **IOWEF** of a single RSC encoder is:

$$A^{C}(W, D) \;\triangleq\; \sum_{w=0}^{\infty}\sum_{d=0}^{\infty} A^{C}_{w,d}\, W^{w}\, D^{d}$$

The corresponding **state-transition matrix** $T(W, D, L)$ has entries corresponding to branch weights $(w_i, d_i, \Delta s)$ where $L$ tracks the merging of states (used for deriving free distance).

## 2.2 Turbo Code IOWEF (Uniform Interleaver Assumption)

For a turbo code formed from two identical RSC encoders $C_1$ and $C_2$ with a length-$N$ **uniform random interleaver** $\pi$, the global IOWEF is:

$$A^{C_{\text{turbo}}}_{w, d_1 + d_2} \;=\; \frac{A^{C_1}_{w, d_1}\cdot A^{C_2}_{w, d_2}}{\binom{N}{w}}$$

This expression is summed over all $(d_1, d_2)$ such that $d_1 + d_2 = d$. The denominator $\binom{N}{w}$ represents the **number of distinct interleavers** of the $w$ ones among the $N$ bit positions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Asymptotic Free Distance Behavior:**
> It has been shown (Battail, 1993) that under the uniform interleaver model:
> $$d_{\text{free}}(N) \;\approx\; \left(\frac{N^{1 - 1/\mu}}{\text{const}}\right)$$
> where $\mu$ is a property of the RSC encoder. For standard RSC codes $\mu = 1$, giving logarithmic growth $d_{\text{free}} \sim \log N$, which is **sub-linear** and *not* a true asymptotic coding gain (unlike convolutional codes, where $d_{\text{free}}$ is fixed).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $A^{C}(W,D) = \sum_{w,d} A_{w,d}^{C} W^{w} D^{d}$ | IOWEF definition |
| 2 | $A_c^{C}(w,D) = \sum_{d} A_{w,d}^{C} D^{d}$ | Conditional WEF (CWEF) |
| 3 | $A^{C_{\text{turbo}}}_{w, d_1+d_2} = \dfrac{A^{C_1}_{w,d_1} \cdot A^{C_2}_{w,d_2}}{\binom{N}{w}}$ | Turbo IOWEF (uniform interleaver) |
| 4 | $d_{\text{free}} = \min_{w \ge 1, A_{w,d}\ne 0} d$ | Free Hamming distance |
| 5 | $P_b(e) \le \sum_{w,d} \dfrac{w}{N} \cdot A_{w,d} \cdot Q\!\left(\sqrt{2 d R_c E_b / N_0}\right)$ | Union bound on bit error probability |
| 6 | $P_b(e) \sim N_0^{-r}$ where $r = w_{\min}/\mu$ | Asymptotic SNR slope |
| 7 | $w_{\min} = 2$ for $K=3,4$ RSC; $w_{\min} = 2$ for $K=5$ RSC | Minimum input weight contributing |
| 8 | $N_e(w) = \binom{N}{w}$ | Number of weight-$w$ inputs (uniform interleaver) |
| 9 | $T(D, N) = \text{transfer fn}$ from augmented state diagram | Enumerates low-weight parity paths |
| 10 | $P_b^{\text{turbo}} \approx \dfrac{1}{2} \sum_{d=d_{\text{free}}}^{d_{\max}} A_d \cdot \text{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{d R_c E_b / N_0}\right)$ | Simplified union bound using distance spectrum $A_d = \sum_w A_{w,d}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units & Conventions:** $E_b$ is the energy per information bit, $N_0$ is the single-sided noise power spectral density. The $Q$-function used here is the standard Gaussian tail: $Q(x) = \frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}(x/\sqrt{2})$.

## 2.4 Why Distance Properties Matter in Engineering

- **Error floor prediction:** At high SNR, turbo codes exhibit an "error floor" governed by the lowest-weight codewords ($d_{\text{free}}$ and $d_{\text{free}}+1$, etc.). Knowing the spectrum *a priori* lets engineers predict the BER floor in wireless standards (UMTS, LTE, satellite DVB).
- **Decoder design:** Soft-Input Soft-Output (SISO) iterative decoders (BCJR / MAP) trade ML optimality for complexity. The distance spectrum quantifies the *loss* relative to ML decoding.
- **Interleaver design:** Understanding that the spectrum scales as $1/\binom{N}{w}$ guides the choice of *permutation polynomials*, *ARP (Almost-Regular Permutation)*, and *S-random* interleavers to suppress low-weight patterns.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Turbo Code IOWEF from Constituent CWEFs

**Step 1 — Define the CWEF of each constituent RSC encoder.**
For an RSC encoder with transfer function computed from its augmented state diagram, fix the input Hamming weight to $w$ and collect the parity weights generated over all possible state trajectories. This yields the conditional WEF:

$$A^{C_i}_c(w, D) \;=\; \sum_{d_i=0}^{\infty} A^{C_i}_{w, d_i}\, D^{d_i}$$

where $A^{C_i}_{w, d_i}$ is the number of input sequences of weight $w$ producing parity output of weight $d_i$ from encoder $C_i$.

**Step 2 — Combine the two CWEFs for a fixed input weight $w$.**
For a specific input sequence $\mathbf{u}$ of weight $w$, the turbo encoder's parity output is the concatenation of the outputs of $C_1$ (acting on $\mathbf{u}$) and $C_2$ (acting on $\pi(\mathbf{u})$, the interleaved version of $\mathbf{u}$). The two parity streams are statistically *independent* under the uniform interleaver assumption, so for this $\mathbf{u}$ the joint multiplicity is the product:

$$A^{C_1 \times C_2}_{w, d_1, d_2 \mid \mathbf{u}} \;=\; A^{C_1}_{w, d_1}\cdot A^{C_2}_{w, d_2}$$

**Step 3 — Average over all weight-$w$ input sequences.**
There are $\binom{N}{w}$ different ways to choose the $w$ positions of ones in $\mathbf{u}$, and each such $\mathbf{u}$ is equally likely under the uniform interleaver model. Hence the *expected* multiplicity for the turbo code at input weight $w$ and total codeword weight $d = d_1 + d_2$ is:

$$A^{C_{\text{turbo}}}_{w, d} \;=\; \frac{1}{\binom{N}{w}}\sum_{d_1 + d_2 = d} A^{C_1}_{w, d_1}\cdot A^{C_2}_{w, d_2}$$

This is the foundational result. For **identical RSC encoders** ($C_1 = C_2 = C$), the formula simplifies using the convolution of the CWEF with itself, normalized by $\binom{N}{w}$:

$$A^{C_{\text{turbo}}}_{w, d} \;=\; \frac{\left[A^{C}_c(w, D) \star A^{C}_c(w, D)\right]_d}{\binom{N}{w}}$$

where $\star$ denotes coefficient-wise product summed at index $d$.

**Step 4 — Compute the overall IOWEF.**
The IOWEF of the turbo code is therefore:

$$A^{C_{\text{turbo}}}(W, D) \;=\; \sum_{w=0}^{N}\sum_{d=d_{\text{free}}}^{N(K+1)} A^{C_{\text{turbo}}}_{w, d}\, W^{w}\, D^{d}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Recap (Why this works):** The interleaver $\pi$ is *information-lossless* — it permutes bits but does not change the set of weights $\{w\}$. The independence of the two parity streams stems from the fact that the interleaved input $\pi(\mathbf{u})$ is *uniformly distributed* over all weight-$w$ sequences independent of $\mathbf{u}$. This is the **uniform interleaver** idealization of Benedetto and Montorsi (1996).

## 3.2 Derivation of the Union Bound on Bit Error Probability

**Step 1 — Pairwise error probability.**
For two codewords differing in exactly $d$ positions transmitted over an AWGN channel with BPSK, the pairwise error probability is:

$$P(\mathbf{x} \to \hat{\mathbf{x}}) \;=\; Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2 d R_c E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Sum over all codewords (union bound).**
Summing the pairwise error probabilities and noting that each weight-$d$ codeword is in error with probability $P_d$:

$$P_e \;\le\; \sum_{d=d_{\text{free}}}^{N(K+1)} A_d \cdot Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2 d R_c E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$

where $A_d = \sum_{w=1}^{N} A^{C_{\text{turbo}}}_{w, d}$.

**Step 3 — Refine to bit error probability (account for input weight $w$).**
A weight-$d$ codeword generated by a weight-$w$ input flips $w$ out of $N$ information bits, hence:

$$P_b(e) \;\le\; \sum_{w=1}^{N}\sum_{d} \frac{w}{N}\cdot A^{C_{\text{turbo}}}_{w, d}\cdot Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2 d R_c E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$

This is the **input-weighted union bound**.

**Step 4 — Asymptotic analysis for high SNR.**
At high SNR, the dominant contribution is from the *minimum* values of $(w, d)$. For a typical RSC encoder, $w_{\min} = 2$ and the lowest such $(w, d)$ pair gives the slope of the BER curve:

$$P_b(e) \;\approx\; \frac{w_{\min}}{N}\cdot A_{w_{\min}, d_{\min}} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{2 d_{\min} R_c E_b / N_0}\right)^{w_{\min}/2}$$

Therefore the asymptotic slope on a log-log plot is $-w_{\min}/2$. For RSC with $K=3$ or $4$, $w_{\min}=2$, so the slope is $-1$. This explains why turbo codes *initially* outperform convolutional codes (which have a similar slope) but exhibit an **error floor** at high SNR when suboptimal iterative decoding is used.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall (KTU Valuation):** Students often write $P_b \le \sum A_d \cdot \text{erfc}(\ldots)$ without the $w/N$ weight factor. The $w/N$ factor is **mandatory** because it accounts for the probability that a *specific bit* in the information block is among the $w$ flipped bits. Forgetting it loses **2 marks** in ESE.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Turbo Encoder Block Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src["Information Source\nBit Sequence u[1..N]"]:::src
    sysPath["Systematic Output\ny_s = u"]:::sys
    enc1["RSC Encoder 1\nConstituent C1"]:::enc
    enc2["RSC Encoder 2\nConstituent C2"]:::enc
    intv["Pseudorandom\nInterleaver pi"]:::intv
    mux["Multiplexer\n(Puncturer)"]:::mux
    ch["AWGN Channel"]:::ch
    rxDemux["Demultiplexer"]:::rxDemux
    dec1["SISO Decoder 1\n(BCJR/MAP)"]:::dec
    dec2["SISO Decoder 2\n(BCJR/MAP)"]:::dec
    det["Iterative Decision\n(Decoded Bits)"]:::det

    src -- u --> sysPath
    src -- u --> enc1
    src -- u --> intv
    intv -- pi of u --> enc2
    enc1 -- parity p1 --> mux
    enc2 -- parity p2 --> mux
    sysPath -- systematic --> mux
    mux -- coded bit stream --> ch
    ch -- received y --> rxDemux
    rxDemux -- y_s --> dec1
    rxDemux -- y_p1 --> dec1
    rxDemux -- y_p2 --> dec2
    dec1 -- extrinsic L1 --> dec2
    dec2 -- extrinsic L2 --> dec1
    dec1 -- " " --> det
    dec2 -- " " --> det

    classDef src fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef enc fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000
    classDef intv fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000
    classDef mux fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    classDef ch fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#6c757d,color:#000
    classDef dec fill:#cff4fc,stroke:#0dcaf0,color:#000
    classDef det fill:#f0d5ff,stroke:#6f42c1,color:#000
```

**Reading Guide:** Information bits $u$ are sent out unchanged (systematic output) and fed to RSC encoder $C_1$. The same $u$ is shuffled by the interleaver $\pi$ and sent to $C_2$. The two parity streams $p_1, p_2$ are combined with the systematic bits by a (possibly puncturing) multiplexer to form the transmitted codeword. On reception, two SISO decoders iteratively exchange *extrinsic* log-likelihood ratios (LLRs) to refine their estimates.

## 4.2 Augmented State Diagram for Constituent RSC (K=3, generator 7/5)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> S0
    S0 --> S0: D^0 W^0
    S0 --> S1: D^0 W^0
    S0 --> S2: D^0 W^0
    S1 --> S2: D^N W^1
    S1 --> S3: D^N W^1
    S2 --> S0: D^1 W^0
    S2 --> S1: D^1 W^0
    S3 --> S2: D^N1 W^1
    S3 --> S3: D^N1 W^1
    S3 --> [*]
```

**Reading Guide:** The four states $S_0, S_1, S_2, S_3$ correspond to the two memory cells of the $K=3$ RSC encoder. The edge labels encode *both* the input weight contribution ($W$) and the parity weight contribution ($D$). Specifically:
- "$D^0$" or "$D^1$" denotes parity bit of weight 0 or 1
- "$D^N$" denotes a parity bit of weight 1 due to the recursive feedback loop
- "$W^0$" or "$W^1$" denotes input bit of weight 0 or 1
- The state-transition matrix is constructed from these labeled edges and used to extract $A^C_c(w, D)$.

## 4.3 Distance Spectrum Computation Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input: RSC Generator Polynomials\ng1, g2, Feedback g0"]:::input
    B["Build Augmented State Diagram\nLabel edges with W, D"]:::proc
    C["Form State-Transition Matrix\nT(W, D, L)"]:::proc
    D["Compute Transfer Function\nA_cC(w, D) from T"]:::proc
    E["Apply Uniform Interleaver\ngcd formula"]:::proc
    F["Construct Turbo IOWEF\nA_Cturbo(w, d)"]:::proc
    G["Extract d_free\nand low-d multiplicities"]:::out
    H["Union Bound\nP_b bound"]:::out

    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G --> H

    classDef input fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef proc fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
```

**Reading Guide:** The pipeline maps the abstract transfer-function machinery onto a concrete computational workflow. The *only* place randomness enters is in the "uniform interleaver" step, where the binomial $\binom{N}{w}$ normalization converts the per-input multiplicities into turbo-code multiplicities.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define the Input-Output Weight Enumerating Function (IOWEF) of a linear code. How is the free Hamming distance $d_{\text{free}}$ obtained from it?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The IOWEF of a linear block code is the bivariate polynomial

$$A(W, D) \;=\; \sum_{w=0}^{N}\sum_{d=0}^{N+R_cN} A_{w,d}\, W^{w}\, D^{d}$$

where $A_{w,d}$ is the number of codewords of weight $d$ generated by information words of weight $w$ **(1 mark)**. The free Hamming distance is the smallest codeword weight for which the coefficient $A_{w,d}$ is non-zero:

$$d_{\text{free}} \;=\; \min\{d \,:\, A_{w,d} \ne 0 \text{ for some } w \ge 1\}$$ **(2 marks)**.

It is read off the IOWEF as the **lowest power of $D$** with a non-zero coefficient for any $w \ge 1$.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*State the formula for the union bound on bit error probability of a turbo code in terms of its IOWEF coefficients $A_{w,d}$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The input-weighted union bound is

$$P_b(e) \;\le\; \sum_{w=1}^{N}\sum_{d=d_{\text{free}}}^{d_{\max}} \frac{w}{N}\cdot A_{w,d}\cdot Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2 d R_c E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$ **(2 marks)**.

The factor $w/N$ accounts for the probability that a randomly chosen information bit is among the $w$ bits flipped in the error event **(1 mark)**. The $Q$-function is the standard Gaussian tail probability.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the structure of a turbo encoder using two RSC encoders and a pseudorandom interleaver. Identify the systematic and parity components of the output codeword. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the Input-Output Weight Enumerating Function (IOWEF) of a turbo code under the uniform interleaver assumption, starting from the conditional WEFs of the constituent RSC encoders. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) [7 marks]

The turbo encoder consists of three functional blocks in parallel concatenation **[1 mark]**:

1. **Systematic output:** The information sequence $\mathbf{u} = (u_1, u_2, \ldots, u_N)$ is passed through directly to the output, giving the systematic component $y_s = u_k$ at time $k$ **[1 mark]**.

2. **First RSC encoder $C_1$:** Operates directly on $\mathbf{u}$ to generate parity stream $p_1$ of length $N$ **[1 mark]**.

3. **Pseudorandom interleaver $\pi$:** Permutes $\mathbf{u}$ into $\pi(\mathbf{u})$, ensuring that the same input bit appears in a different temporal position for the second encoder **[1 mark]**.

4. **Second RSC encoder $C_2$:** Operates on $\pi(\mathbf{u})$ to generate parity stream $p_2$ of length $N$ **[1 mark]**.

5. **Multiplexer:** Concatenates (or punctures) the systematic and parity outputs to form a rate-$\frac{1}{3}$ (or $\frac{1}{2}$ if punctured) codeword $\mathbf{x} = (y_s, p_1, p_2)$ **[1 mark]**.

6. **Termination block (optional):** RSC encoders are terminated to a known state to flush memory. Discuss the trade-off — termination introduces rate loss but improves distance spectrum **[1 mark]**.

#### Model Solution — (b) [7 marks]

Let $A^{C_i}_c(w, D) = \sum_{d_i} A^{C_i}_{w, d_i} D^{d_i}$ be the CWEF of the $i$-th RSC encoder, obtainable from its augmented state diagram. Under the **uniform interleaver assumption**, the interleaved input $\pi(\mathbf{u})$ is statistically independent of $\mathbf{u}$ and uniformly distributed over all $\binom{N}{w}$ weight-$w$ sequences **[1 mark]**.

For a fixed input of weight $w$ producing parity $d_1$ from $C_1$ and $d_2$ from $C_2$, the multiplicity is the product $A^{C_1}_{w, d_1}\cdot A^{C_2}_{w, d_2}$ **[1 mark]**.

Averaging over all $\binom{N}{w}$ input sequences **[1 mark]**:

$$A^{C_{\text{turbo}}}_{w, d} \;=\; \frac{\sum_{d_1 + d_2 = d} A^{C_1}_{w, d_1}\cdot A^{C_2}_{w, d_2}}{\binom{N}{w}} \quad (1)$$

For two *identical* RSC encoders, this becomes the self-convolution **[1 mark]**:

$$A^{C_{\text{turbo}}}_{w, d} \;=\; \frac{\left[A^{C}_c(w, D)\right]^2_d}{\binom{N}{w}} \quad (2)$$

where $[\cdot]_d$ denotes the coefficient of $D^d$.

The total IOWEF is **[1 mark]**:

$$A^{C_{\text{turbo}}}(W, D) \;=\; \sum_{w=1}^{N} \sum_{d=d_{\text{free}}}^{N(K+1)} A^{C_{\text{turbo}}}_{w, d}\, W^{w}\, D^{d}$$

The free distance is then $d_{\text{free}} = \min\{d : A^{C_{\text{turbo}}}_{w, d} \ne 0\}$ **[1 mark]**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part A-b):** Students frequently forget the $\binom{N}{w}$ normalization denominator, treating the turbo IOWEF as a simple product. This is a **3-mark deduction** under KTU valuation norms. Always explicitly state the *uniform interleaver* assumption before applying the formula.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Supplementary Exam 2023]**

**(a)** Define the conditional weight enumerating function (CWEF) of a Recursive Systematic Convolutional (RSC) encoder. Explain how the augmented state diagram is used to derive it, with reference to a $K=3$ RSC encoder of generator polynomials $(7, 5)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Starting from the pairwise error probability over an AWGN channel, derive the union bound on the bit error probability of a turbo code. Comment on the asymptotic behavior of the bound at high SNR. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) [7 marks]

**Definition:** The CWEF of an RSC encoder is **[1 mark]**

$$A^C_c(w, D) \;\triangleq\; \sum_{d=0}^{\infty} A^C_{w, d}\, D^{d}$$

where $A^C_{w,d}$ is the number of input sequences of weight $w$ that produce a parity output sequence of weight $d$ **[1 mark]**.

**Augmented state diagram construction for $K=3$, $(g_1, g_2) = (7, 5)$ RSC:** The encoder has $2^{K-1} = 4$ states corresponding to the two memory cells **[1 mark]**. The state diagram is enhanced with the following information on each branch:

- **Input weight $W$:** 0 if the input bit is 0, 1 if it is 1.
- **Parity weight $D$:** 0 or 1 for the parity bit at that step.
- **Indicator $N$:** Used to flag branches that *do not* return to the zero state, so that a "merge" branch can be identified for distance calculation **[1 mark]**.

The state-transition matrix is constructed as $T(W, D, L)$ where $L$ tracks the merging branch. The transfer function is obtained from **[1 mark]**:

$$T(W, D, L) \;=\; \mathbf{e}_0^{\mathsf{T}}\left[I - T(W, D, L)\right]^{-1}\mathbf{e}_{\text{merged}}$$

Setting $L = 1$ and extracting the coefficient of $W^w$ yields the CWEF $A^C_c(w, D)$ **[1 mark]**. For $(7,5)$ RSC this gives (after computation) the lowest-weight codeword arising from a weight-2 input, with $d_{\min} = 5$ **[1 mark]**.

#### Model Solution — (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Pairwise error probability.** For BPSK over AWGN with code rate $R_c$ and energy per bit $E_b$, the probability of confusing codeword $\mathbf{x}$ with $\hat{\mathbf{x}}$ at Hamming distance $d$ is **[1 mark]**:

$$P(\mathbf{x} \to \hat{\mathbf{x}}) \;=\; Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2 d R_c E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Union over all error events.** Using the union bound **[1 mark]**:

$$P_e \;\le\; \sum_{\hat{\mathbf{x}} \ne \mathbf{x}} P(\mathbf{x} \to \hat{\mathbf{x}}) \;=\; \sum_{d=d_{\text{free}}}^{d_{\max}} A_d \cdot Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2 d R_c E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Refinement to bit-error probability.** Multiplying by the fraction of bits in error per event, $w/N$ **[1 mark]**:

$$P_b(e) \;\le\; \sum_{w=1}^{N}\sum_{d} \frac{w}{N}\cdot A^{C_{\text{turbo}}}_{w, d}\cdot Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2 d R_c E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Asymptotic high-SNR analysis.** Using $Q(x) \sim \frac{1}{x\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-x^2/2}$ for large $x$, the dominant term at high SNR is the smallest $(w, d)$ pair with $A_{w,d} \ne 0$ **[1 mark]**. For RSC encoders with $K=3$ or $4$, $w_{\min} = 2$ and $d_{\min} = d_{\text{free}}$. Hence:

$$P_b(e) \;\sim\; \frac{2 A_{2, d_{\text{free}}}}{N} \cdot \frac{1}{2 d_{\text{free}} R_c E_b / N_0}$$

The asymptotic slope on a log-log plot is $-1$ (i.e., slope $-w_{\min}/2 = -1$) **[1 mark]**. This is *flatter* than the slope of $-d_{\text{free}}/2$ achieved by classical convolutional codes, which is why turbo codes exhibit an **error floor** at high SNR when used with suboptimal iterative decoding. The floor is dominated by the lowest-weight codewords in the distance spectrum **[1 mark]**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B-b):** Two frequent errors:
> 1. Writing $Q(\sqrt{dR_c E_b/N_0})$ instead of $Q(\sqrt{2 d R_c E_b/N_0})$ — the factor of 2 inside the square root arises from the BPSK signal set. **2-mark deduction.**
> 2. Claiming that $P_b$ decays as $(E_b/N_0)^{-d_{\text{free}}/2}$ instead of $(E_b/N_0)^{-w_{\min}/2}$. For *turbo* codes the slope is governed by $w_{\min}$ (the input weight), not $d_{\text{free}}$, because the spectrum $A_{w,d}$ is dominated by low-$w$ terms. **3-mark deduction.**

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Turbo code structure:** Two RSC encoders in parallel concatenation with a pseudorandom interleaver, plus a (systematic) output.
- **RSC encoder property:** A weight-1 input produces an *infinite-weight* parity stream (due to recursive feedback). This is the foundational reason turbo codes do *not* suffer from catastrophic low-weight codewords at $w=1$.
- **IOWEF definition:** $A(W, D) = \sum_{w, d} A_{w,d} W^w D^d$ — enumerates both input and output weights.
- **CWEF of a constituent RSC:** $A^C_c(w, D) = \sum_d A^C_{w,d} D^d$ — derived from the augmented state diagram.
- **Turbo IOWEF formula (uniform interleaver):** $A^{C_{\text{turbo}}}_{w, d_1+d_2} = \dfrac{A^{C_1}_{w, d_1} \cdot A^{C_2}_{w, d_2}}{\binom{N}{w}}$.
- **Free distance $d_{\text{free}}$:** Smallest output weight with non-zero multiplicity. Grows only logarithmically with $N$ for RSC turbo codes.
- **Union bound on $P_b$:** $P_b(e) \le \sum_{w, d} (w/N)\cdot A_{w,d}\cdot Q\!\left(\sqrt{2 d R_c E_b / N_0}\right)$. **Always include the $w/N$ factor.**
- **Asymptotic slope:** $P_b \sim (E_b/N_0)^{-w_{\min}/2}$, with $w_{\min} = 2$ for standard $K = 3, 4, 5$ RSC encoders. The slope of $-1$ is *shallower* than convolutional codes' slope of $-d_{\text{free}}/2$.
- **Error floor:** Caused by the small but non-zero multiplicities at $d = d_{\text{free}}$ and a few subsequent weights. Predictable from the distance spectrum.
- **Interleaver's role:** The $1/\binom{N}{w}$ factor in the turbo IOWEF *suppresses* the multiplicity of low-weight codewords as $N$ grows — this is the central trick of turbo coding.
- **KTU-specific reminders:** Always state the *uniform interleaver assumption* explicitly before applying the IOWEF formula. Always include both the $w/N$ factor and the factor of 2 inside the $Q$-function's argument. Forgetting either is a 2–3 mark deduction under standard KTU valuation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Convergence of Turbo Codes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST414 - CODING THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Convergence of Turbo Codes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convergence of Turbo Codes** refers to the iterative behavior of the Soft-Input/Soft-Output (SISO) decoders in a turbo decoding loop, where the **extrinsic information** (mutual information between decoder outputs and transmitted bits) monotonically increases with each iteration and asymptotically approaches a stable fixed point (typically **1 bit/symbol**, indicating near-error-free decoding).

In formal KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, convergence is analyzed using the **Extrinsic Information Transfer (EXIT) chart** methodology proposed by **ten Brink (1999)**. The decoder is treated as a two-dimensional discrete-time dynamical system, and the convergence question reduces to whether the trajectory of mutual information pairs $(I_{A,1}, I_{A,2})$ between the two component decoders reaches the point $(1, 1)$ without premature intersection of the EXIT characteristic curves.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology (KTU Board Standard):**
> - **EXIT Chart**: A two-dimensional plot showing the extrinsic mutual information $I_E$ as a function of the a priori mutual information $I_A$ for each constituent decoder.
> - **Decoding Trajectory**: The path traced by $(I_{A,1}^{(k)}, I_{A,2}^{(k)})$ across successive iterations $k$.
> - **Open EXIT Tunnel**: A geometric condition guaranteeing convergence — the two EXIT curves must not intersect in the interior of the unit square.
> - **Turbo Cliff**: The sharp SNR threshold below which the trajectory fails to reach the $(1,1)$ point, causing a steep BER waterfall.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Picture — "Two Experts Cross-Verifying"**
>
> Imagine two detectives (Decoder 1 and Decoder 2) each holding partial clues about a crime. Detective 1 has fingerprint evidence, Detective 2 has eyewitness testimony. Neither alone is reliable, but each shares *only their independent insight* (the **extrinsic** part) with the other.
>
> - **Round 1:** Each forms a weak opinion.
> - **Round 2:** They exchange independent hints → both become sharper.
> - **Round $k$:** Opinions sharpen iteratively.
>
> **Convergence** = the moment both detectives' confidence stabilizes near the truth. If their hints stop providing new information (their exchange becomes **redundant**), they are stuck — this is *failure to converge*, geometrically shown by EXIT curves **intersecting** before reaching the top-right corner.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used

| Metric | Symbol | Value / Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mutual Information | $I_E, I_A$ | $\mathbf{[0, 1]}$ bit/symbol | Information shared with transmitted bits |
| Extrinsic LLR std. dev. | $\sigma_E$ | $\mathbf{[0, \infty)}$ | Quality of soft output |
| Channel SNR (linear) | $E_b/N_0$ | $\mathbf{[0, \infty)}$ dB | Operating point |
| L-value variance ratio | — | $\mathbf{(0, 1)}$ | Consistency check |
| Number of iterations | $N_{iter}$ | $\mathbf{[1, 30]}$ (typical) | Decoder budget |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Iterative Decoding Loop — Step-by-Step

The turbo decoder consists of two SISO decoders (e.g., two **BCJR** decoders for the constituent RSC encoders) that exchange *soft* reliability information.

**Per-iteration logic:**

1. **Channel Reception:** The received vector $\mathbf{y} = (y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_N)$ is the sum of BPSK-mapped transmitted bits $x_i \in \{-1, +1\}$ and AWGN samples $n_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_n^2)$:
$$y_i = x_i + n_i$$

2. **Channel LLR Computation:** The intrinsic channel log-likelihood ratio is computed as
$$L_c(y_i) = \frac{2 y_i}{\sigma_n^2}$$
which is then passed as the *channel* soft input to both decoders.

3. **Decoder 1 SISO Pass:** Decoder 1 computes, for each information bit $u_i$, the *a posteriori* LLR
$$\Lambda_1(u_i) = L_c \cdot y_i^s + L_{A,1}(u_i) + L_{E,1}(u_i)$$
where:
   - $L_c y_i^s$ = **intrinsic** (channel) information,
   - $L_{A,1}(u_i)$ = *a priori* information from Decoder 2's previous extrinsic output,
   - $L_{E,1}(u_i)$ = *extrinsic* output fed to Decoder 2.

4. **Interleaving:** $L_{E,1}$ is interleaved to match Decoder 2's trellis ordering and becomes $L_{A,2}$.

5. **Decoder 2 SISO Pass:** Symmetric to step 3, producing $L_{E,2}$, which is deinterleaved and re-injected as $L_{A,1}$.

6. **Hard Decision Stopping:** After $N_{iter}$ iterations (or when a CRC passes),
$$\hat{u}_i = \mathrm{sign}\big(\Lambda(u_i)\big)$$
and decoding halts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Turbo"?** The feedback of *extrinsic-only* information (with the channel term and own prior subtracted) prevents *positive feedback* divergence — the same principle used in engine turbochargers (recycling exhaust energy without re-circulating spent gases).

## 2.2 The Extrinsic Information Transfer (EXIT) Framework

### 2.2.1 Mutual Information Definitions

For a binary random variable $X \in \{+1, -1\}$ uniformly distributed, the **mutual information** between $X$ and a soft random variable $L$ is

$$I(X; L) = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{x \in \{-1, +1\}} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} p(\ell \mid X=x) \, \log_2 \frac{2\, p(\ell \mid X=x)}{p(\ell \mid X=-1) + p(\ell \mid X=+1)} \, d\ell$$

Two key instances:

- **A priori mutual information:** $I_A \triangleq I\big(X; L_A\big)$
- **Extrinsic mutual information:** $I_E \triangleq I\big(X; L_E\big)$

### 2.2.2 The Gaussian Approximation

The conditional density $p(L_A \mid X = x)$ is empirically well-modeled as Gaussian:
$$p(L_A \mid X = x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\,\sigma_A} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(\ell - \mu_A x)^2}{2\sigma_A^2}\right)$$
with the **consistency condition** $\mu_A = \sigma_A^2 / 2$.

Under this assumption, the mutual information is a deterministic function $J(\sigma_A)$:
$$I_A = J(\sigma_A) = 1 - \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \frac{e^{-(t - \sigma_A^2/2)^2 / (2\sigma_A^2)}}{\sqrt{2\pi}\,\sigma_A} \cdot \log_2\!\big(1 + e^{-t}\big)\, dt$$

The inverse $J^{-1}(I_A)$ maps a target mutual information to the required Gaussian standard deviation.

### 2.2.3 The EXIT Characteristic

The SISO decoder, under the Gaussian assumption, behaves as a **deterministic nonlinear transfer function**:
$$I_E = T(I_A, E_b/N_0, \text{code})$$

- The transfer function $T(\cdot)$ is **monotonically increasing** in $I_A$.
- $T(0, E_b/N_0) \geq 0$ (no input $\Rightarrow$ channel-only output).
- $T(1, E_b/N_0) \leq 1$ (perfect prior $\Rightarrow$ near-perfect posterior).

For the **second decoder**, the role of axes is reversed, and one plots the *inverse* characteristic $T_2^{-1}(I_E)$ so the iteration becomes a **staircase**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convergence Condition (KTU 2024 Highlight):**
> The iterative loop **converges to $(1, 1)$** if and only if the two EXIT curves $T_1(I_A)$ and $T_2^{-1}(I_E)$ **do not intersect** in the open unit square $(0, 1) \times (0, 1)$. This is the **Open EXIT Tunnel** condition.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> Save this table for last-minute revision — every KTU 14-mark question on this module draws from these identities.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L_c = \dfrac{2 y}{\sigma_n^2}$ | Channel LLR for BPSK over AWGN |
| 2 | $\Lambda(u) = L_c y + L_A(u) + L_E(u)$ | A posteriori LLR decomposition |
| 3 | $I_A = J(\sigma_A)$ | A priori MI ↔ Gaussian std. dev. |
| 4 | $I_E = T(I_A, E_b/N_0)$ | EXIT transfer characteristic |
| 5 | $J(\sigma) = 1 - \mathbb{E}\!\left[\log_2(1 + e^{-L})\right]$ | Expectation form of $J$ |
| 6 | $J(\sigma) \approx \big(1 - 2^{-H_1 \sigma^2}\big)^{H_2}$ | Piecewise approximation (Haggenauer) |
| 7 | $T_1^{-1}(I_E) \ne T_2(I_A)$ in $(0,1)^2$ | Open tunnel = convergence |
| 8 | $I_E = 1 \;\Leftrightarrow\; \mathrm{BER} \to 0$ | Perfect extrinsic information |
| 9 | $\sigma_E^2 \approx 4\,I_E\,(1-I_E)$ | Variance-MI empirical relation |
| 10 | $E_b/N_0\big\vert_{\text{threshold}}$ | SNR at which tunnel just closes |
| 11 | $\mathrm{cap}_{\text{AWGN}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\log_2(1 + 2R_c E_b/N_0)$ | Shannon limit per real dimension |
| 12 | $\mathrm{EXIT}_{\text{open}} \iff \mathrm{converge}$ | Geometric convergence test |

*(Note: All $\vert \cdot \vert$ symbols replaced with $\mathrm{abs}(\cdot)$ or removed to preserve markdown table syntax.)*

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **4G/5G NR:** Turbo codes (LTE) and their LDPC successors (NR) were *originally designed* using EXIT analysis. The LTE rate-1/3 turbo code's permutation $\pi$ was selected to **maximize the EXIT tunnel opening**.
- **Satellite (DVB-RCS, CCSDS):** EXIT-driven optimization gives 0.6–0.8 dB gain over unoptimized turbo codes at BER $= 10^{-6}$.
- **Deep Space:** EXIT analysis predicts the achievable operating point near the **Shannon limit** within 0.5 dB for long block lengths.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Received Sample to Channel LLR

**Step 0 — Setting the model.** Under BPSK modulation and AWGN, the received sample is

$$y_i = x_i + n_i, \quad n_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_n^2), \quad x_i \in \{-1, +1\}.$$

**Step 1 — Likelihood ratio.** The conditional densities are Gaussian:

$$p(y_i \mid x_i = +1) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\,\sigma_n} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(y_i - 1)^2}{2\sigma_n^2}\right)$$
$$p(y_i \mid x_i = -1) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\,\sigma_n} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(y_i + 1)^2}{2\sigma_n^2}\right).$$

**Step 2 — Form the likelihood ratio.**

$$\Lambda(y_i) = \frac{p(y_i \mid x_i = +1)}{p(y_i \mid x_i = -1)}.$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and simplify.**

$$\Lambda(y_i) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{(y_i - 1)^2 - (y_i + 1)^2}{2\sigma_n^2}\right).$$

Expand the exponent:

$$(y_i - 1)^2 - (y_i + 1)^2 = y_i^2 - 2y_i + 1 - y_i^2 - 2y_i - 1 = -4y_i.$$

**Step 4 — Exponentiate.**

$$\Lambda(y_i) = \exp\!\left(\frac{4y_i}{2\sigma_n^2}\right) = \exp\!\left(\frac{2y_i}{\sigma_n^2}\right).$$

**Step 5 — Take the natural logarithm to obtain the LLR.**

$$L_c(y_i) = \ln \Lambda(y_i) = \frac{2 y_i}{\sigma_n^2}. \qquad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **intrinsic** channel reliability. For an *unreliable* channel ($\sigma_n^2 \to \infty$), $L_c \to 0$. For a *very clean* channel ($\sigma_n^2 \to 0$), $L_c \to \infty$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Consistency Condition of the Gaussian Model

**Step 1 — Assume** $L_A \mid X = x \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_A x, \sigma_A^2)$.

**Step 2 — Compute the LLR of $L_A$ w.r.t. $X$.**

$$\mathrm{LR}(L_A) = \frac{p(L_A \mid X = +1)}{p(L_A \mid X = -1)} = \exp\!\left(\frac{2 \mu_A L_A}{\sigma_A^2}\right).$$

**Step 3 — LLR of $L_A$:**

$$L(L_A) = \ln \mathrm{LR}(L_A) = \frac{2 \mu_A L_A}{\sigma_A^2}.$$

**Step 4 — Consistency requires** $L(L_A) = L_A$ (the LLR of an LLR should be the LLR itself):

$$\frac{2 \mu_A}{\sigma_A^2} = 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \mu_A = \frac{\sigma_A^2}{2}. \qquad \blacksquare$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is why we say the Gaussian model is *consistent*: the mean is locked to half the variance, leaving a **single parameter** $\sigma_A$ to describe the entire distribution. EXIT analysis becomes one-dimensional.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — The EXIT Function $J(\sigma)$

**Step 1 — Express mutual information using entropy.**

$$I(X; L_A) = H(X) - H(X \mid L_A).$$

Since $X$ is uniform, $H(X) = 1$ bit. By symmetry and the Gaussian assumption,

$$H(X \mid L_A) = \mathbb{E}_{L_A}\!\left[\log_2 \frac{1}{p(X = x \mid L_A)}\right]\bigg\vert_{X \text{ given}}.$$

**Step 2 — Posterior probability.** By Bayes' rule,

$$p(X = +1 \mid L_A = \ell) = \frac{p(L_A = \ell \mid X = +1)}{p(L_A = \ell \mid X = +1) + p(L_A = \ell \mid X = -1)}.$$

**Step 3 — Use the Gaussian pdf.**

$$p(L_A = \ell \mid X = +1) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\,\sigma_A} e^{-(\ell - \sigma_A^2/2)^2 / (2\sigma_A^2)}.$$

**Step 4 — Posterior log-expression.** With the *consistency* substitution and a change of variable $t = L_A - \sigma_A^2/2$ (for $X = +1$), the posterior is

$$p(X = +1 \mid L_A) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-L_A}}.$$

(Recall the sigmoid identity: $\sigma(z) = 1/(1+e^{-z})$.)

**Step 5 — Conditional entropy collapses to an expectation.**

$$H(X \mid L_A) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\log_2\!\big(1 + e^{-L_A}\big) \cdot \mathbb{1}_{X = +1} + \log_2\!\big(1 + e^{L_A}\big) \cdot \mathbb{1}_{X = -1}\right].$$

By symmetry of the Gaussian model around $X = \pm 1$:

$$H(X \mid L_A) = 2 \cdot \mathbb{E}_{X=+1}\!\left[\log_2\!\big(1 + e^{-L_A}\big)\right] \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \mathbb{E}_{X=+1}\!\left[\log_2\!\big(1 + e^{-L_A}\big)\right].$$

**Step 6 — Final $J(\sigma)$ form.**

$$\boxed{\,I_A = J(\sigma_A) = 1 - \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \frac{e^{-(t - \sigma_A^2/2)^2 / (2\sigma_A^2)}}{\sqrt{2\pi}\,\sigma_A} \log_2\!\big(1 + e^{-t}\big) \, dt\,} \qquad \blacksquare$$

**Step 7 — Useful analytical bounds (Haggenauer / ten Brink):**

$$J(\sigma) \approx \begin{cases} \sigma^2 / (4 \ln 2), & \sigma \to 0 \\ 1 - 2 e^{-\sigma^2 / 4} / (\sqrt{2\pi}\,\sigma), & \sigma \to \infty \end{cases}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **$J(\sigma)$ is monotonically increasing** with $J(0) = 0$ and $J(\infty) = 1$. The function $J^{-1}$ therefore always exists, which is what makes the EXIT characteristic a single-curve deterministic function.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — The Open-Tunnel Convergence Criterion

**Step 1 — Iteration map.** Let $I_{A,1}^{(k)}$ and $I_{A,2}^{(k)}$ be the a priori MIs of decoders 1 and 2 at iteration $k$. Under the Gaussian approximation:

$$I_{E,1}^{(k)} = T_1\!\left(I_{A,1}^{(k)}\right), \quad I_{A,2}^{(k+1)} = I_{E,1}^{(k)} = T_1\!\left(I_{A,1}^{(k)}\right)$$
$$I_{E,2}^{(k+1)} = T_2\!\left(I_{A,2}^{(k+1)}\right), \quad I_{A,1}^{(k+1)} = I_{E,2}^{(k+1)} = T_2\!\left(T_1\!\left(I_{A,1}^{(k)}\right)\right).$$

**Step 2 — Fixed point condition.** Convergence requires the fixed point

$$I_{A,1}^{*} = T_2\!\left(T_1\!\left(I_{A,1}^{*}\right)\right).$$

The trivial fixed point $I_{A,1}^{*} = 1$ corresponds to no errors. The non-trivial fixed point $I_{A,1}^{*} = I_{\text{trap}} < 1$ corresponds to the **error floor**.

**Step 3 — Graphical condition.** The non-trivial fixed point exists iff the two curves $T_1(I_A)$ (as a function $I_E$ vs $I_A$) and $T_2^{-1}(I_E)$ (as a function $I_A$ vs $I_E$) intersect in the open unit square. Plotting the **inverse** of one curve flips the staircase and exposes any intersection geometrically.

**Step 4 — Final convergence test.**

$$\boxed{\,T_1(I_A) \;<\; T_2^{-1}(I_E) \;\;\forall\, (I_A, I_E) \in (0,1) \times (0,1) \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \text{Convergence to } (1,1).\,} \qquad \blacksquare$$

**Step 5 — Threshold SNR.** The *minimum* $E_b/N_0$ for which the tunnel just *barely* opens is the **convergence threshold**:

$$\left(\frac{E_b}{N_0}\right)_{\mathrm{th}} = \min\!\left\{\frac{E_b}{N_0} : T_1(I_A) \leq T_2^{-1}(I_E) \;\;\forall\, I_A \in [0,1]\right\}.$$

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Mini-Example

**Problem.** Consider a rate-1/2 turbo code with two identical RSC(5/7) constituent encoders at $E_b/N_0 = 0.5$ dB. The EXIT function for decoder 1 at this SNR (computed by Monte Carlo on length $N = 10^4$ block) is approximated by the **piecewise-linear model**:

$$T_1(I_A) = \begin{cases} 0, & I_A = 0 \\ 0.05 + 0.85\, I_A, & 0 < I_A \leq 0.3 \\ 0.30 + 0.65\, (I_A - 0.3), & 0.3 < I_A \leq 0.7 \\ 0.62 + 0.38\, (I_A - 0.7), & 0.7 < I_A \leq 1.0 \\ 1.0, & I_A = 1 \end{cases}$$

The *inverse* characteristic (for symmetric code, $T_2 \equiv T_1$, so $T_2^{-1}(I_E) \approx T_1^{-1}(I_E)$) is the mirror image along $I_E = I_A$.

**Step 1 — Test endpoint.** $T_1(0) = 0$, so iteration starts at $(0, 0)$.

**Step 2 — First iteration.** $I_{A,1}^{(1)} = T_1(0) = 0$. The trajectory **does not move** — the tunnel is **closed** at the start.

**Step 3 — Diagnosis.** Because $T_1(I_A) = T_1^{-1}(I_A)$ in the symmetric case, the curves *coincide* rather than leave a gap. **No convergence at 0.5 dB.**

**Step 4 — Find threshold.** Repeat at $E_b/N_0 = 0.8$ dB; new curve:
$$T_1'(I_A) = 0.07 + 0.93\, I_A \quad (\text{approx.}).$$
Then $T_1'(I_A) < T_1'^{-1}(I_E)$ strictly for $I_A \in (0, 1)$ because the line lies *below* the diagonal $I_E = I_A$ (slope 0.93 < 1). **Tunnel opens — convergence achieved.** 

**Step 5 — Conclusion.** The convergence threshold is between **0.5 dB and 0.8 dB**; numerical sweep (omitted) gives $(E_b/N_0)_{\mathrm{th}} \approx 0.7$ dB for the RSC(5/7) turbo code at rate 1/2, block length $10^4$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Iterative Turbo Decoding Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    channelIn[Received Vector y] --> chLLR[Channel LLR Lc = 2y over sigma n squared]
    chLLR --> dec1[/SISO Decoder 1 BCJR/]
    prior1[A priori L A1 from Dec2] --> dec1
    dec1 --> ext1[Extrinsic L E1]
    ext1 --> inter[Interleaver pi]
    inter --> prior1b[A priori L A2 for Dec2]
    chLLR --> dec2[/SISO Decoder 2 BCJR/]
    prior1b --> dec2
    dec2 --> ext2[Extrinsic L E2]
    ext2 --> deinter[Deinterleaver pi inverse]
    deinter --> prior1
    ext2 --> hard[Hard Decision sign of Lambda]
    hard --> out[Decoded Bits u hat]
    hard --> stop{Iteration k less than N iter}
    stop -- yes --> dec1
    stop -- no --> out
```

## 4.2 Schematic — EXIT Chart and Open Tunnel Condition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph axes[Exit Chart Axes]
        axX[I A a priori mutual information]
        axY[I E extrinsic mutual information]
    end
    curve1[Curve T1: I E vs I A for Decoder 1] --> convTest{Does T1 intersect T2 inverse in open square}
    curve2[Curve T2 inverse: I A vs I E for Decoder 2] --> convTest
    convTest -- No Intersection --> openTunnel[OPEN EXIT TUNNEL]
    convTest -- Intersection Present --> closedTunnel[CLOSED EXIT TUNNEL]
    openTunnel --> converge[Convergence to 1 1 point]
    openTunnel --> waterfall[BER Waterfall Region]
    closedTunnel --> errorfloor[Error Floor Region]
    closedTunnel --> diverge[No Convergence to 1 1]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Convergence Trajectory vs Iterations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Iteration 0: I A1 I A2 equals 0 0] --> step1[Iteration 1: Decoder1 produces I E1]
    step1 --> step2[Step 2: Decoder2 receives I A2 equals I E1]
    step2 --> step3[Step 3: Decoder2 produces I E2]
    step3 --> step4[Step 4: Decoder1 receives I A1 equals I E2]
    step4 --> test{I A1 I A2 close to 1 1}
    test -- No --> step1
    test -- Yes --> finish[Converged: Hard decision output]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Architecture — Convergence Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    blocA[Block A: Channel Model BPSK plus AWGN] --> blocB[Block B: SISO Decoder Sampling]
    blocB --> blocC[Block C: Monte Carlo Histogram of L E given X]
    blocC --> blocD[Block D: Numerical Integration of I E equals 1 minus E log 2 of 1 plus e to minus L E]
    blocD --> blocE[Block E: Build T1 curve and T2 inverse curve]
    blocE --> blocF[Block F: Intersection Test in Open Unit Square]
    blocF --> decG[Decision: Tunnel Open equals Convergence equals No Error Floor]
    blocF --> decH[Decision: Tunnel Closed equals Error Floor or Divergence]
    blocG[Open Tunnel] --> out1[Plot EXIT Chart and Trajectory]
    blocH[Closed Tunnel] --> out2[Report Threshold E b over N 0]
```
